title: The New Heaven and New Earth

sections.header: Verses 1-8 — "New Creation and the Dwelling of God"

heb.gloss: a new heaven and a new earth
heb.paragraph: The adjective kainos (new in quality, renewed) rather than neos (new in time, brand-new) is theologically significant. Kainos implies transformation and renewal rather than replacement from scratch. The phrase echoes Isaiah 65:17 and 66:22 (LXX), where God promises to create 'new heavens and a new earth.' The theological question is whether this is a renovation of the existing creation or an entirely new creation ex nihilo. The use of kainos favors continuity-in-transformation: the new creation fulfills rather than abolishes the old, just as the resurrection body fulfills rather than replaces the mortal body (1 Cor 15:42–44).

heb.word: ἡ σκηνὴ τοῦ θεοῦ μετὰ τῶν ἀνθρώπων
heb.transliteration: hē skēnē tou theou meta tōn anthrōpōn
heb.gloss: the tabernacle/dwelling of God is with humanity
heb.paragraph: The noun skēnē (tent, tabernacle, dwelling) is cognate with the Hebrew shĕkhīnāh (the divine presence that dwelled in the tabernacle and temple). The Incarnation was described with the same root: eskēnōsen en hēmin (he tabernacled among us, John 1:14). The new creation fulfills the trajectory that began with the tabernacle in the wilderness (Exod 25:8), continued in Solomon's temple (1 Kgs 8:27), was embodied in Christ (John 1:14; 2:19–21), and now reaches its consummation in God's unmediated dwelling with humanity. The mediating structures—tabernacle, temple, incarnate body—are no longer needed because God's presence is direct and universal.

panels.ctx: Revelation 21:1–8 is the climactic vision of the entire Bible—the consummation toward which the narrative of creation, fall, redemption, and judgment has been moving since Genesis 1. The new heaven and new earth replace the first heaven and first earth, which have 'passed away' (apēlthan, v. 1). The absence of the sea (v. 1) is symbolically significant: in Jewish apocalyptic tradition and throughout Revelation, the sea represents chaos, evil, and the origin of the beast (13:1). Its disappearance signals the eradication of all chaotic, threatening forces from the renewed creation. The New Jerusalem descends from heaven 'prepared as a bride beautifully dressed for her husband' (v. 2), merging the city and bride metaphors that have run through chapters 17–21. The great declaration from the throne—'Look! God's dwelling place is now among the people' (v. 3)—fulfills the covenant formula that echoes from Leviticus 26:11–12 through Jeremiah 31:33 and Ezekiel 37:27. God's promise to 'wipe every tear from their eyes' (v. 4) quotes Isaiah 25:8, and the catalogue of abolished evils—death, mourning, crying, pain—constitutes the most comprehensive promise of eschatological restoration in Scripture. The divine self-identification 'I am the Alpha and the Omega' (v. 6) forms an inclusio with 1:8, spanning the entire book. The final contrast between 'the one who is victorious' (v. 7) and the list of those consigned to the lake of fire (v. 8) reaffirms the binary choice that has run throughout Revelation.

cross.note: Isaiah's promise of 'new heavens and a new earth' provides the primary Old Testament background for Revelation 21:1. Isaiah's vision includes the end of weeping and distress, infant mortality abolished, and divine presence filling the renewed creation. Revelation 21:1–4 universalizes and intensifies this Isaianic vision: not merely a reformed Jerusalem but an entirely renewed cosmos where God dwells directly with humanity.

Isaiah prophesied that God 'will swallow up death forever. The Sovereign LORD will wipe away the tears from all faces.' Revelation 21:4 quotes this promise verbatim and extends it: not only tears but death itself, mourning, crying, and pain are abolished. The eschatological hope of Isaiah finds its complete fulfillment in the new creation.

God's covenant promise—'I will put my dwelling place among you... I will walk among you and be your God, and you will be my people'—is the foundational text behind the great declaration of Revelation 21:3. The entire biblical narrative of God's progressive self-revelation—tabernacle, temple, incarnation—reaches its consummation in God's unmediated dwelling with humanity in the new creation.

Paul declared, 'If anyone is in Christ, the new creation has come.' Revelation 21:5 ('I am making everything new') reveals the cosmic scope of what Paul experienced individually in Christ. The new creation that begins in the believer's regeneration reaches its full expression in the renewal of heaven and earth.

notes.note: The new heaven and new earth are the eternal state—the permanent dwelling of the redeemed following the millennium and final judgment. The old creation, corrupted by sin and subjected to futility (Rom 8:20–22), is replaced by a creation purged of all effects of the Fall. The absence of the sea is literal: the new earth will have no oceans. The sea in Scripture is associated with chaos, separation, and the origin of evil (the beast arose from the sea, 13:1). Its elimination signals the permanent removal of all threat and disorder.

The New Jerusalem descending from heaven is not a natural development of human civilization but a divine gift—it comes 'down out of heaven from God.' The bridal imagery ('prepared as a bride') identifies the city with the Lamb's wife (v. 9; 19:7–8). The great declaration from the throne—'God's dwelling place is now among the people'—is the fulfillment of every tabernacle, temple, and incarnation promise in Scripture. God is no longer mediated through structures or institutions; He is directly, permanently, personally present with His people.

God will personally 'wipe every tear from their eyes.' This is the most tender image of divine compassion in Scripture—the sovereign Lord of the universe stoops to the intimate act of drying a human face. Death, mourning, crying, and pain are abolished—not suppressed or managed but eliminated from the fabric of reality. 'The old order of things has passed away' is the most comprehensive statement of eschatological transformation in the Bible.

The declaration 'I am making everything new' (v. 5) is one of only two direct speeches of God from the throne in Revelation (the other being 1:8). Its rarity underscores its importance: this is God's own commentary on the new creation. The Alpha and Omega title (v. 6) spans all of history; the spring of the water of life (v. 6) echoes John 4:14 and 7:37–39. The list of those excluded (v. 8)—the cowardly, unbelieving, vile, murderers, sexually immoral, sorcerers, idolaters, and liars—identifies the character traits that are incompatible with the new creation.

The new creation is not an escape from materiality but its perfection. Calvin insisted against Platonic dualism that the physical world is good because God made it (Gen 1:31), and the eschatological hope is not the soul's liberation from the body but the redemption of the entire created order (Rom 8:19–22). The absence of the sea symbolizes the end of all that is threatening, unstable, and chaotic—the creation is finally at rest. The covenant formula of verse 3 ('They will be his people, and God himself will be with them and be their God') is the telos of all theology: the purpose of creation, redemption, and consummation is divine-human communion.

That God declares 'I am making everything new' from the throne confirms that the new creation is a sovereign act, not a gradual evolution. Calvin would note the present tense: God is making, not merely will make. The process of renewal that began with Christ's resurrection is ongoing and will reach its consummation in the new heaven and earth. The exclusion of the faithless (v. 8) is not arbitrary but the logical consequence of a creation defined by the presence of God: those who refuse God's presence cannot inhabit a reality constituted by that presence.

The adjective kainos (new) rather than neos (new) emphasizes qualitative newness—transformation in character rather than mere temporal succession. The LXX of Isaiah 65:17 uses kainous and kainēn, the same terms John employs. The clause hē thalassa ouk estin eti (the sea is no more) uses the existential ouk estin with the temporal adverb eti, indicating permanent cessation of existence rather than temporary absence.

The phrase hē skēnē tou theou meta tōn anthrōpōn (the dwelling of God is with humanity) contains a textual variant: some manuscripts read laoi (peoples, plural) instead of laos (people, singular). The plural reading—'they will be his peoples'—is strongly attested and theologically significant, indicating that ethnic diversity is not abolished in the new creation but fulfilled. God's presence encompasses all nations, not a single homogeneous group.

Beale reads the new creation as the fulfillment of the entire biblical creation-fall-redemption-consummation narrative. The new heaven and earth are not a replacement of the old but a transformation—as kainos indicates—just as the resurrection body is a transformation of the mortal body, not a different body. The absence of the sea symbolizes the eradication of the chaotic, threatening forces that have operated throughout the old creation: the sea from which the beast arose (13:1), the sea that separates and destroys, the primordial chaos of Genesis 1:2 that God ordered but did not eliminate. Now, with the final elimination of evil, the sea itself is unnecessary. The covenant formula of verse 3 is the structural center of the passage and arguably of the entire book: everything in Revelation moves toward this declaration of unmediated divine presence.

The Alpha and Omega self-designation creates a literary inclusio with 1:8, framing the entire book as a single revelation of God's purpose: from beginning to end, God is making all things new. Beale notes that the 'spring of the water of life' (v. 6) echoes the Ezekielian temple river (Ezek 47:1–12) and anticipates the river of life in 22:1–2, creating a water-of-life motif that symbolizes the life-giving presence of God flowing to His people. The exclusion list of verse 8 corresponds to the warnings in the letters to the seven churches (chs. 2–3), confirming that Revelation's ethical demands are not mere exhortation but eschatological reality: those who persist in these behaviors are excluded from the new creation.

Osborne identifies 21:1–22:5 as the climactic vision of Revelation and the theological apex of the entire biblical canon. The structure parallels and reverses Genesis 1–3: where Genesis describes creation followed by fall, Revelation describes judgment followed by new creation. The New Jerusalem descending from heaven (v. 2) fulfills the expectation of a heavenly city that appears throughout the Bible (Gal 4:26; Heb 11:10, 16; 12:22; 13:14). The bridal imagery merges the city and the church into a single symbol, indicating that the redeemed community is the new creation's defining reality. The abolition of death, mourning, crying, and pain (v. 4) reverses the curses of Genesis 3 and fulfills the prophetic promises of Isaiah 25:8 and 35:10.

Osborne notes that God speaks directly from the throne only twice in Revelation: 1:8 and 21:5–8. Both speeches are self-revelatory declarations that frame the book's theological content. The command 'Write this down, for these words are trustworthy and true' (v. 5) parallels 19:9 and 22:6, forming a chain of authorial authenticity assertions that guarantee the reliability of the entire Apocalypse.

heb.word: τὴν νύμφην, τὴν γυναῖκα τοῦ ἀρνίου
heb.transliteration: tēn nymphēn, tēn gynaika tou arniou
heb.gloss: the bride, the wife of the Lamb
heb.paragraph: The double designation nymphē (bride) and gynē (wife) merges the anticipatory and consummated aspects of the covenant relationship. The bride is the one preparing for the wedding (19:7–8); the wife is the one in completed union. Both terms applied to a single entity—the New Jerusalem—indicate that the city is not a structure but a community: the people of God in their perfected, eternal relationship with the Lamb. The genitive tou arniou (of the Lamb) identifies Christ as the divine bridegroom, fulfilling the Old Testament imagery of God as Israel's husband (Hos 2:16–20; Isa 54:5–8).

panels.ctx: The detailed description of the New Jerusalem (vv. 9–21) is introduced by one of the seven bowl angels—the same group that introduced the prostitute vision (17:1)—creating a deliberate structural parallel between the two women: Babylon the prostitute and Jerusalem the bride. The city descends from heaven 'with the glory of God' (v. 11), and its brilliance is compared to jasper, 'clear as crystal.' The twelve gates bear the names of the twelve tribes (v. 12), and the twelve foundations bear the names of the twelve apostles (v. 14), uniting Old and New Testament Israel in a single architectural symbol. The angel's measurement of the city (vv. 15–17) reveals it as a perfect cube—1,400 miles (12,000 stadia) in each dimension. The only other cubic structure in Scripture is the Most Holy Place (1 Kgs 6:20), indicating that the entire city is now the Holy of Holies—the place of God's unmediated presence. The walls are jasper, the city is pure gold 'as pure as glass' (v. 18), and the twelve foundations are adorned with precious stones (vv. 19–20) that recall the high priest's breastplate (Exod 28:17–20). The twelve gates are single pearls, and the street is transparent gold (v. 21). Every architectural detail communicates theological reality: this is not merely a beautiful city but the perfected dwelling of God with humanity, where every barrier between the divine and the human has been removed.

cross.note: Ezekiel's temple vision provides the primary structural template for the New Jerusalem description: angelic guide, measuring rod, detailed dimensions, and the flowing of God's glory into the completed structure. However, John's vision transcends Ezekiel's: where Ezekiel described a renewed temple within a city, John describes a city that is itself the temple—no separate sacred space is needed because the entire city is the Holy of Holies.

Solomon's Most Holy Place was a perfect cube—twenty cubits long, wide, and high. The New Jerusalem's cubic shape (12,000 stadia in each dimension) identifies the entire city as the eschatological Holy of Holies: a space defined by the unmediated presence of God. What was a single room in Solomon's temple now encompasses the entire redeemed community.

The twelve precious stones adorning the high priest's breastplate, each engraved with the name of a tribe of Israel, are echoed in the twelve foundation stones of the New Jerusalem (Rev 21:19–20). The priestly breastplate symbolized Israel's representation before God; the city's foundations symbolize the permanent establishment of God's people in His presence.

notes.note: The angel's invitation—'Come, I will show you the bride, the wife of the Lamb'—directly parallels 17:1 ('Come, I will show you the punishment of the great prostitute'). This structural pairing emphasizes the contrast between the two cities: Babylon the prostitute and Jerusalem the bride. The city's radiance 'like a most precious jewel' reflects the unmediated glory of God Himself dwelling within it.

The twelve gates named for the twelve tribes of Israel and the twelve foundations named for the twelve apostles unite the old and new covenants in a single, permanent structure. The wall's purpose is not defensive (there are no enemies) but symbolic—it defines the community and establishes the identity of those who belong to it. Israel and the church are not separate peoples but one people of God, united in the eternal city.

The city is a perfect cube—12,000 stadia (approximately 1,400 miles) in each dimension. This is not merely symbolic; it describes the actual dimensions of the eternal dwelling. The cubic shape recalls the Most Holy Place of Solomon's temple (1 Kgs 6:20), indicating that the entire city is the Holy of Holies—the place of God's direct, unmediated presence. The wall of 144 cubits (v. 17) may represent 12 x 12, symbolizing the fullness of God's people.

The building materials—jasper walls, gold streets, jeweled foundations, pearl gates—describe a city of incomprehensible beauty and value. These are not metaphors but literal descriptions of the eternal city's material composition, which transcends anything in present creation. The transparent gold of the street suggests a material reality so pure that it achieves a quality unknown in the present order.

The structural parallel between the prostitute's introduction (17:1) and the bride's introduction (21:9) reveals that history presents only two ultimate allegiances: Babylon or Jerusalem, the corrupt world system or the redeemed community. Calvin would emphasize that these are not merely future realities but present spiritual orientations. Every church, every believer, is always choosing between Babylon's allure and Jerusalem's promise. The union of the twelve tribes and twelve apostles in a single structure affirms the unity of God's people across all ages and dispensations—there is one Israel of God, one bride, one city.

The cubic dimensions identify the New Jerusalem as the eschatological Holy of Holies—not a room within a temple but an entire city defined by the presence of God. Calvin would see here the fulfillment of the Reformation's most radical claim: that every believer has direct access to God, without mediating priesthood or institutional structure. In the new creation, this access is permanent, universal, and unobstructed. The precious stones and transparent gold are not mere luxury but theological symbols: the city's material substance reflects the glory of the God who indwells it.

The measurement dodekastadioustadiōn dekadyo chilialdōn (twelve thousand stadia) presents a city of approximately 1,400 miles (2,200 km) in each dimension. The number 12,000 (12 x 1,000) combines the covenant number (12) with the completeness number (1,000), producing a symbolically 'perfect' measurement. The clause to mēkos kai to platos kai to hypsos autēs isa estin (its length and width and height are equal) explicitly identifies the shape as a cube, the only other cubic sacred space in the Bible being the debir of Solomon's temple (1 Kgs 6:20).

The twelve foundation stones correspond roughly but not exactly to the twelve gems of the high priest's breastplate (Exod 28:17–20). The identification of ancient gemstones with modern equivalents is uncertain; the NET note observes that iaspis (jasper), sappheiros (sapphire/lapis lazuli), chalkedōn (chalcedony), smaragdos (emerald), sardonyks (sardonyx), sardion (carnelian), chrysolithos (chrysolite/peridot), bēryllos (beryl), topazion (topaz), chrysoprasos (chrysoprase), hyakinthos (jacinth/zircon), and amethystos (amethyst) represent a spectrum of colors creating a rainbow effect around the city's base.

Beale reads the New Jerusalem as the anti-Babylon: every detail of the prostitute's description in chapter 17 is countered by the bride's description in chapter 21. The prostitute was adorned with gold, precious stones, and pearls (17:4)—external decoration masking internal corruption; the bride is constructed of gold, precious stones, and pearls (21:18–21)—material substance expressing intrinsic holiness. The prostitute sat on seven hills (17:9); the bride rests on twelve foundations (21:14). The prostitute was a haunt of demons (18:2); the bride is the dwelling of God (21:3). The cubic shape recalls not only the Holy of Holies but also the new-creation theology of Isaiah 65–66: the entire city is sacred space because the entire creation has been renewed. The precious stones of the foundations (vv. 19–20) allude to both the high priest's breastplate (Exod 28) and the king of Tyre's Edenic adornment (Ezek 28:13), suggesting that the New Jerusalem recovers the Edenic glory that was lost through the Fall.

Osborne identifies the bowl angel's appearance as guide (v. 9) as the structural device that creates the deliberate contrast between prostitute (17:1–19:10) and bride (21:9–22:9). The two women represent two cities, two systems, two destinies—and the reader must choose between them. The city's impossible dimensions (1,400 miles cubed) indicate that literal interpretation must be balanced with symbolic awareness: the measurements communicate theological truths about completeness, perfection, and the fulfillment of covenant promises rather than architectural specifications. The twelve-gate, twelve-foundation structure unites Israel and the church, confirming that God's redemptive purpose encompasses both Testaments' peoples in a single eschatological community.

sections.header: Verses 22-27 — "No Temple, for God and the Lamb Are Its Temple"

heb.gloss: I did not see a temple in it
heb.paragraph: The statement ouk eidon naon (I did not see a temple) is one of the most theologically revolutionary declarations in the Bible. The entire Old Testament cultus—tabernacle, temple, priesthood, sacrifice—existed to mediate God's presence to His people. The absence of a temple does not indicate God's absence but His overwhelming, unmediated presence: the Lord God Almighty and the Lamb are the temple (ho naos autēs). The mediating institution is superseded by the mediated reality. This is the ultimate fulfillment of Jesus' declaration that He would destroy the temple and raise it in three days (John 2:19–21)—the temple is now a Person, and in the new creation, that Person fills all things.

panels.ctx: The declaration that the city has no temple (v. 22) is the theological climax of the New Jerusalem vision and perhaps of the entire Bible's temple theology. From the Garden of Eden (God's first dwelling with humanity) through the tabernacle, Solomon's temple, the Second Temple, the incarnation ('the Word became flesh and tabernacled among us'), and the church ('you are the temple of the living God'), Scripture has traced a progressive trajectory of divine presence. Now that trajectory reaches its telos: God and the Lamb are the temple, and no mediating structure is needed. The absence of sun and moon (v. 23) echoes Isaiah 60:19–20 and does not indicate their non-existence but their irrelevance—the glory of God provides all the light the city needs. The nations walking by its light (v. 24) and kings bringing their splendor into it (v. 24) suggest that ethnic and cultural diversity is not abolished but redeemed and offered to God. The perpetually open gates (v. 25) indicate absolute security—there is no threat, no enemy, no night to fear. The exclusion of the impure (v. 27) is not defensive but definitional: the new creation is constituted by holiness, and nothing unholy can exist within it.

cross.note: Isaiah prophesied: 'The sun will no more be your light by day, nor will the brightness of the moon shine on you, for the LORD will be your everlasting light, and your God will be your glory.' Revelation 21:23 fulfills this promise: the city needs no sun or moon because the glory of God illuminates it and the Lamb is its lamp.

Jesus declared, 'Destroy this temple, and I will raise it again in three days.' John explains that 'the temple he had spoken of was his body.' Revelation 21:22 consummates this temple theology: God and the Lamb are the city's temple, and the entire New Jerusalem is the space of unmediated divine presence that the physical temple could only foreshadow.

Isaiah's vision of the nations streaming to Jerusalem's light and kings bringing their wealth provides the background for Revelation 21:24–26. The eschatological Jerusalem fulfills what historical Jerusalem anticipated: a center of divine glory that draws all nations to worship and offer their best to God.

notes.note: The absence of a temple is not a deficiency but a fulfillment. Every temple in Israel's history—tabernacle, Solomon's temple, the Second Temple—was a provisional structure pointing forward to God's unmediated dwelling with His people. In the new creation, the provisional gives way to the permanent: God and the Lamb are the temple. The entire city is sacred space; there is no distinction between sacred and secular because the divine presence permeates everything.

The city has no need of sun or moon—not because they do not exist but because the glory of God outshines them. The Lamb is the city's lamp, providing light for the nations to walk by. The presence of 'nations' and 'kings' bringing their glory suggests that ethnic and political diversity is not erased in the eternal state but redeemed and consecrated. The cultural achievements of humanity, purified of sin, find their proper place as offerings to God.

The gates are never shut because there is no night—no darkness, no threat, no danger. Absolute security characterizes the eternal state. The exclusion of 'nothing impure' and those who practice 'shameful' or 'deceitful' things is not restrictive legalism but ontological reality: the new creation is constituted by the holiness of God, and nothing incompatible with that holiness can exist within it.

The abolition of the temple is the consummation of the Reformation's central insight: that the mediation of God's presence through institutional structures is provisional and ultimately gives way to direct divine-human communion. Calvin, who devoted enormous energy to reforming worship by removing what he saw as human additions to God's prescribed worship, would see in verse 22 the ultimate vindication of the regulative principle: when God's presence is direct and unmediated, all humanly devised worship structures become unnecessary.

The nations bringing their glory into the city suggests that cultural production, artistic achievement, and human creativity are not destroyed but redeemed. Calvin affirmed common grace—God's gifts to all humanity regardless of election—and here the fruits of common grace find their eternal home. What was created under the old order, insofar as it reflects divine glory, is gathered into the new creation. But nothing impure enters: the purification is total, and only what honors God survives the transition.

The phrase ho gar kyrios ho theos ho pantokratōr naos autēs estin kai to arnion (for the Lord God Almighty is its temple, and the Lamb) coordinates God and the Lamb as joint temple. The singular naos (temple) with a compound subject (God and the Lamb) uses a Christologically significant construction: the Father and the Son together constitute the single sacred space. This is functional Trinitarianism expressed architecturally.

The phrase ei mē hoi gegrammenoi en tō bibliō tēs zōēs tou arniou (except those written in the Lamb's book of life) uses the conditional exception clause (ei mē) to define entry into the city. The perfect participle gegrammenoi (having been written) indicates prior, completed action—names were inscribed before entry, not at the moment of entry. This is consistent with the election theology of Revelation (13:8; 17:8).

Beale reads the temple's absence as the culmination of the Bible's entire temple theology. The trajectory runs from Eden (God's original dwelling with humanity) through the patriarchal altars, Sinai tabernacle, Solomon's temple, the post-exilic temple, the incarnation (John 1:14; 2:19–21), the church (1 Cor 3:16; Eph 2:19–22), to the new creation where God and the Lamb are the temple. Each stage represented an intensification of divine presence; the final stage eliminates the mediating structure entirely because the reality it mediated—God's presence—now fills the entire city. The open gates and the nations' procession (vv. 24–26) fulfill the Isaianic vision of the eschatological pilgrimage of the nations (Isa 2:2–4; 60:1–14), confirming that salvation is not ethnically restricted but universally available. The exclusion of the impure (v. 27) is not a threat but a promise: the new creation is defined by holiness, and those within it are permanently secure in their purity.

Osborne identifies the no-temple declaration as the most radical theological claim in the entire New Jerusalem vision: the institutional mediation of God's presence, which defined Israel's worship for over a millennium, is not merely reformed but abolished—superseded by the direct, unmediated presence of God and the Lamb. This does not diminish the temple's historical significance but reveals its teleological purpose: the temple was always pointing beyond itself to the reality of direct divine-human communion. The nations' pilgrimage with their 'glory and honor' (vv. 24, 26) reflects a positive theological anthropology: human cultural achievement, purified of sin, has a place in the eternal order. The new creation does not negate creation but fulfills it.

themes.note: Revelation 21 is the theological climax of the Bible, where covenant and holiness reach their ultimate expression. God's dwelling with humanity fulfills every covenant promise from Abraham through the new covenant. Mercy triumphs as tears, death, and pain are abolished. The absence of a temple declares that unmediated divine presence is the defining reality of the new creation.

rows.label: v.1
rows.text: New heaven and new earth; sea is no more

rows.label: v.2
rows.text: New Jerusalem descends as a bride

rows.label: vv.3-4
rows.text: God dwells with humanity; tears, death, pain abolished

rows.label: vv.5-8
rows.text: Alpha and Omega speaks; overcomers and the excluded

rows.label: vv.9-11
rows.text: Bowl angel shows the bride; city of divine glory

rows.label: vv.22-23
rows.text: No temple; God and the Lamb are the temple; no sun needed

rows.label: vv.24-27
rows.text: Nations walk by its light; gates never shut; nothing impure enters

lit.note: Revelation 21 moves from cosmic renewal (vv. 1–8) to city description (vv. 9–21) to temple theology (vv. 22–27), with each movement intensifying the theme of divine presence. The structural parallel with the prostitute vision (17:1–19:10) creates a comprehensive contrast between the two women/cities, forcing the reader to choose between Babylon's allure and Jerusalem's glory.

hebtext.gloss: a new heaven and a new earth
hebtext.note: The adjective kainos (new in quality, renewed) rather than neos (new in time, brand-new) is theologically significant. Kainos implies transformation and renewal rather than replacement from scratch. The phrase echoes Isaiah 65:17 and 66:22 (LXX), where God promises to create 'new heavens and a new earth.' The theological question is whether this is a renovation of the existing creation or an entirely new creation ex nihilo. The use of kainos favors continuity-in-transformation: the new creation fulfills rather than abolishes the old, just as the resurrection body fulfills rather than replaces the mortal body (1 Cor 15:42–44).

hebtext.word: ἡ σκηνὴ τοῦ θεοῦ μετὰ τῶν ἀνθρώπων
hebtext.tlit: hē skēnē tou theou meta tōn anthrōpōn
hebtext.gloss: the tabernacle/dwelling of God is with humanity
hebtext.note: The noun skēnē (tent, tabernacle, dwelling) is cognate with the Hebrew shĕkhīnāh (the divine presence that dwelled in the tabernacle and temple). The Incarnation was described with the same root: eskēnōsen en hēmin (he tabernacled among us, John 1:14). The new creation fulfills the trajectory that began with the tabernacle in the wilderness (Exod 25:8), continued in Solomon's temple (1 Kgs 8:27), was embodied in Christ (John 1:14; 2:19–21), and now reaches its consummation in God's unmediated dwelling with humanity. The mediating structures—tabernacle, temple, incarnate body—are no longer needed because God's presence is direct and universal.

hebtext.word: τὴν νύμφην, τὴν γυναῖκα τοῦ ἀρνίου
hebtext.tlit: tēn nymphēn, tēn gynaika tou arniou
hebtext.gloss: the bride, the wife of the Lamb
hebtext.note: The double designation nymphē (bride) and gynē (wife) merges the anticipatory and consummated aspects of the covenant relationship. The bride is the one preparing for the wedding (19:7–8); the wife is the one in completed union. Both terms applied to a single entity—the New Jerusalem—indicate that the city is not a structure but a community: the people of God in their perfected, eternal relationship with the Lamb. The genitive tou arniou (of the Lamb) identifies Christ as the divine bridegroom, fulfilling the Old Testament imagery of God as Israel's husband (Hos 2:16–20; Isa 54:5–8).

hebtext.gloss: I did not see a temple in it
hebtext.note: The statement ouk eidon naon (I did not see a temple) is one of the most theologically revolutionary declarations in the Bible. The entire Old Testament cultus—tabernacle, temple, priesthood, sacrifice—existed to mediate God's presence to His people. The absence of a temple does not indicate God's absence but His overwhelming, unmediated presence: the Lord God Almighty and the Lamb are the temple (ho naos autēs). The mediating institution is superseded by the mediated reality. This is the ultimate fulfillment of Jesus' declaration that He would destroy the temple and raise it in three days (John 2:19–21)—the temple is now a Person, and in the new creation, that Person fills all things.
